original_abstract:
Many Americans express a blend of conservative and liberal views across different policy issues. While traditional research characterizes these voters as cross-pressured, an influential study, “Moderates” (Fowler et al. 2023), argues that they actually hold centrist views on individual issues. To support this, Fowler et al. develop a mixture model designed to identify voters whose preferences are well-summarized by a single liberal-conservative dimension. Because the model classifies the vast majority of voters as ideological "Downsians," the authors conclude that those with centrist estimated scores must be "genuine moderates" who support middle-of-the-road policies.

In this article, we show that this method systematically overstates the prevalence of ideological consistency. The "Moderates" framework defaults to classifying voters as ideological unless they either answer questions randomly or belong to a single, specific cluster of non-ideologues with a shared set of idiosyncratic views. Through simulations and analysis of the original data, we demonstrate that this approach frequently mischaracterizes cross-pressured voters as moderate ideologues simply because their mixed responses do not fit the narrow definition of the model's non-ideological category. Consequently, the conclusion that these voters support centrist policy remains in doubt. We suggest that distinguishing between genuine centrist views and idiosyncratic issue mixes requires richer data—specifically panel studies and more granular policy scales—rather than the interpretation of binary cross-sectional survey responses.
humanized_abstract:
Many Americans hold somewhat conservative and somewhat liberal views on different policy issues, and this small mismatch in their attitudes has been regarded as evidence that they are cross-pressured by traditional research. We show, however, that Americans who seem to be inconsistent in their policy attitudes have consistent attitudes about policy issues when they are moderate, and therefore must be genuine moderates. Our evidence comes from modeling voter attitudes as a mixture of consistent and incoherent individuals. We use a probabilistic mixture model (Lange, Turlach, and Revenaz 2000, Lloyd 2002; Eilers and Goos 2006; Bhansali and Koops 2015, 2016; and Pigeon and Neelon 2017) of a standard, latent factor model that can identify some voters who have attitudes that are easy to summarize using a single ideological “liberal-conservative” Downsian dimension. The model identifies about 88 percent of the U.S. public as such Downsians. We show that voters with centrist estimated positions on the single underlying dimension must be the genuine moderates, the group of voters who hold moderate positions on the moderates issues they care about.

Ideological Consistency as a Faulty Assumption The method we discussed in the previous section for assessing the level of ideological consistency among the public appears to yield an inflated assessment of the level of ideological consistency in the public’s views. Because the Moderates model classifies voters as being ideological by default and treats as non-ideological only those voters whose responses are either randomly or aberrantly clustered, our simulation and the re-examination of the 2012 survey data show that moderate voters with mixed cross-pressured views can be turned into ideological moderates because their mixed views do not fit into the prescribed non-ideological category of voters. In other words, the assumption of the Moderates model, which we used to assess the level of ideological consistency in the public’s views, is clearly inappropriate in the case of moderate voters who hold cross-pressured views. Assessing centrist ideology and understanding the public’s support for moderation accordingly requires a more refined approach than is possible based on survey measures that are not designed to make the appropriate distinctions. In particular, assessing centrist ideology requires panel surveys in which public opinion can be examined in a more nuanced fashion than is possible based on the single Yes-No or 4-point and 5-point binary survey questions that are typically available.

document.output:

document.input:
Many Americans express a blend of conservative and liberal views across different policy issues.
